planting.note: Plant bulbs with the neck at or just above the soil surface in moist, moderately to richly fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; Queen Emma lily is a tropical species from Mauritius and adjacent islands that has been long cultivated in warm climates for its striking burgundy-purple-overlaid foliage and deep rose-pink, fragrant flowers. In zones 9b–11 it can remain in the ground year-round; protect or overwinter as a container plant in zone 9a. All parts are toxic if ingested.

directions: Plant bulbs (neck exposed) in moist, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; Queen Emma lily is among the most dramatically foliaged of all crinum species — the broad, strap-shaped, glossy leaves are heavily suffused with deep burgundy-purple to mahogany-brown pigmentation that creates a rich, dark-toned foliage effect unlike the all-green leaves of most crinum species. This striking burgundy leaf coloring combined with the flowers — large, fragrant, deep rose-pink to wine-rose, lily-like funnels in umbels of 8–15 blooms per scape in summer — creates a bold, tropical composition of dark foliage and deep-rose flowers of unmistakably exotic character. It is a species of Mauritius and the Mascarene Islands in the tropical Indian Ocean, long grown in historic tropical and subtropical gardens worldwide under the name 'Queen Emma lily' after a Hawaiian queen who grew it extensively. The large bulbs develop into impressive clumps over time, producing multiple flower scapes in summer. Outstanding as a container specimen or tropical-effect border plant in zone 9 Gulf Coast gardens where its heat-loving character, tolerance of summer humidity, and dramatic dark foliage make it a premier ornamental bulb.

planting.note: Plant bulbs with the top of the neck at or just above the soil surface in very well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly alkaline to neutral, sandy or rocky soil in full sun; Corsican pancratium is a spring-blooming species (not summer like P. maritimum) with glaucous, gray-green, strap-shaped leaves that are often winter-present. It is more cold-tolerant than P. maritimum and flowers in late spring (May–June). Distinguished from P. maritimum by its earlier bloom time, glaucous leaves, and mountain origins in Corsica, Sardinia, and the Adriatic coast.

directions: Plant bulbs in very well-drained, lean, slightly alkaline to neutral, rocky or sandy soil in full sun; Illyrian sea daffodil is a Mediterranean narcissus relative from the rocky limestone mountains, coastal cliffs, and dry hillsides of Corsica, Sardinia, and the eastern Adriatic coast — flowering in late spring (May–June) rather than late summer like the more familiar P. maritimum, and forming attractive clumps of glaucous, gray-blue-green, strap-shaped leaves that persist through winter providing cool-season interest. The flowers are large, white, fragrant, and distinctly narcissus-like in form but with the characteristic pancratium 'daffodil corona' structure — a prominent, fringed or scalloped white cup at the center surrounded by six long, spreading, pure-white perianth segments — carried in umbels of 8–15 flowers per scape in late spring. The fragrance is powerful and sweet, filling the spring garden air. Like all pancratiums, it is a Mediterranean Summer-dry bulb requiring the well-drained, lean, dry conditions of Mediterranean rocky landscapes and withholding of water in summer dormancy. More cold-tolerant than P. maritimum, it suits zones 7–10 garden climates well. An excellent but underused spring-flowering white bulb for dry, sunny Mediterranean-style borders.

planting.note: Plant bulbs 4–6 inches deep in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; Sprenger's tulip is the latest of all tulip species to bloom (June in northern gardens) and is among the very best naturalizers — spreading freely by seed in garden conditions when allowed to set seed without cutting. A single planting builds into large colonies over 5–10 years. Bulbs are small and difficult to source; seed or specialist tulip nurseries are the main supply route.

directions: Plant bulbs in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in part shade; Sprenger's tulip has two unique distinctions that set it apart from all other tulip species: it is the latest to bloom (flowering in late May through June after all other tulips have finished, bridging the gap between spring bulbs and summer perennials), and it is arguably the best naturalizer in the tulip genus — self-seeding freely in garden conditions to build large, spreading colonies of plants that increase reliably year after year without lifting, dividing, or replanting. The flowers are a vivid, warm, scarlet-red to deep orange-red (with occasional yellow-flushed outer petal surfaces), starry, six-petaled, pointed-oval in bud and opening to a broad, open, star-like display of satiny red in bright sun. For a species that blooms so late and self-seeds so freely, it is perplexingly rare in general cultivation — partly because the small bulbs are difficult to produce commercially and mostly available through specialist sources and from seed. A colony established from seed or a first planting of small bulbs gradually becomes a self-sustaining treasure in the garden, the late-season red tulip display filling a slot in the spring bulb calendar that nothing else occupies. Naturalized colonies have been established in old gardens and appear completely at home in the borderline-shade of deciduous tree canopy.

planting.note: Plant bulbs 3 inches deep in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; two-toned grape hyacinth produces only a single broad, strap-shaped leaf per bulb (unlike the multiple narrow leaves of M. armeniacum) and flowers in mid-spring, later than many grape hyacinths. It naturalizes freely in suitable conditions. The bicolor effect of dark-violet lower florets and sky-blue sterile upper florets on the same spike is unique among muscari.

directions: Plant bulbs in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in part shade; two-toned grape hyacinth stands apart from other muscari species through a striking, built-in bicolor effect on each individual spike: the lower portion of the densely packed raceme carries the typical, deep, dark-blue-violet to navy-blue, urn-shaped, fertile florets of the grape hyacinth type, while the upper portion of the same spike carries distinctly lighter, sky-blue to pale-blue, sterile florets that are more elongated and open — creating a natural two-toned spike that grades from dark violet at the base to pale sky-blue at the tip. This gradation of two distinct blues on a single small flower spike is botanically unique among commonly grown muscari and creates a more complex, interesting flower display than the single-toned spikes of M. armeniacum. A single broad leaf (vs the several narrow leaves of other species) gives the foliage a distinctive, lush quality. The bulbs increase steadily and naturalize in part shade under deciduous trees and shrubs, where the bicolor spring spikes create a particularly attractive display in the dappled light. An outstanding, underused grape hyacinth for the woodland garden.

planting.note: Plant small bulbs 3 inches deep in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; Sardinian glory-of-the-snow is the most uniformly, deeply blue of the chionodoxas — the small white eye of C. luciliae is absent or nearly so, making the entire flower a uniform, intense, gentian-blue. It naturalizes freely by seed and bulb offset, spreading into large colonies in suitable conditions under deciduous trees and in thin turf.

directions: Plant small bulbs in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in part shade; Sardinian glory-of-the-snow is distinguished from the more commonly grown C. luciliae (forbesii) by its flower color: while C. luciliae has a conspicuous white center eye creating a blue-and-white bicolor pattern, C. sardensis is an intense, uniform, all-blue — a clear, deep, gentian-blue to rich cornflower-blue throughout each small, star-shaped, six-petaled flower with only the slightest trace of white at the very center, if any. The effect of a mass planting or naturalized colony in early spring is therefore a more uniformly intense blue carpet than C. luciliae provides — the even, unwavering, gentian-blue creating an extraordinarily pure, concentrated blue ground-color effect under deciduous trees before the canopy leafs out. It appears early (February–March), pushing through snow if needed (the genus name means 'snow-glory'), and naturalizes freely by self-seeding and offset multiplication into large colonies over years. An excellent and somewhat underused alternative or companion to C. luciliae for mass spring-blue naturalizing in woodland gardens, lawn edges, and rough grass under deciduous trees. Hardy to zone 3.
